meta.name: Valora Dashboard Theme
meta.description: High-level design system guidelines inspired by the Valora AI Revenue Optimizer dashboard. Use this file to generate UI that visually matches the provided reference: calm, professional, data-dense, and highly readable.
meta.primaryFontNote: Assume the Google Sans font is globally loaded via the provided <link> tags. All text should default to this font stack unless explicitly overridden.
meta.source: Dashboard screenshot provided by user (AI Pricing Optimizer / Valora).
usageGuidelines: Always prefer semantic tokens (colors, spacing, typography names) from this file over hard-coded values.
When generating components, reference the component definitions here to keep structure, spacing, and hierarchy consistent.
Avoid introducing new visual styles (colors, radii, shadows) unless they clearly extend existing tokens.

colors.description: Semantic color tokens that create a soft, neutral, professional analytics dashboard. Colors are approximate matches to the reference and are intended as guidelines, not strict brand codes.

typography.description: Use Google Sans as the global font to convey a clean, modern, product-like feel. Hierarchy is created via size, weight, and color, not decorative fonts.

fontFamilies.monospace: ui-monospace, SFMono-Regular, Menlo, Monaco, Consolas, "Liberation Mono", "Courier New", monospace

display-lg.usage: Primary page titles such as 'AI Revenue Optimizer Overview'. Limited to one per page.

display-md.usage: Section headers above large groups of cards or charts.

title-sm.usage: Subtitles and section labels inside cards, nav group labels.

body.usage: Default body copy for descriptions and supporting text.

label.usage: Uppercase or small label text, small metric captions, chip labels.

kpi.usage: Key metrics such as large revenue numbers or main KPIs.

rules: Use the primary font family for all text in the app shell, navigation, cards, charts, and controls.
Avoid mixing in additional fonts; rely on weight, color, and size to create hierarchy.
Keep card titles at title-lg and never larger than the page title.
Use muted or subtle text colors for secondary and tertiary information to keep emphasis on the main KPIs.

spacing.description: Spacing is consistent and moderate, enabling high information density while keeping the interface calm and breathable.
usageGuidelines: Use 24–32px padding for outer page gutters and between the sidebar and main content.
Use 20–24px internal padding for primary cards and modules.
Use 12–16px vertical spacing between stacked elements within a card.
Keep spacing between related text elements tight (4–8px) to visually group them.
Maintain consistent gaps between cards in a grid (16–24px).

radius.description: Rounded corners are soft but not fully pill-shaped, creating a friendly yet professional look.
usageGuidelines: Cards and major surfaces use md radius.
Sidebar search inputs and simple inputs use sm radius.
Chips, pills, and badges use pill radius.
Charts can be embedded inside cards without additional radius; rely on the card container.

shadows.description: Shadows are subtle and primarily used to separate cards from the light page background.
usageGuidelines: Use the card shadow for all main information cards against the page background.
Use none for nested elements within a card to avoid visual noise.
Use popover shadow for floating panels like menus or date pickers.
Use focusRaised sparingly for key interactive surfaces when they are actively focused.

borders.description: Borders are thin and soft, primarily used as separators and subtle framing, not heavy outlines.
usageGuidelines: Use 1px subtle borders around cards only when needed for definition; otherwise rely on shadow and background contrast.
Use divider color for separators inside cards and tables.
Avoid strong borders around the sidebar; blend it gently with the page background.

layout.description: High-level page and grid structure guidelines for dashboard-style screens.

rules: Keep content centered with comfortable outer padding on large screens.
Ensure a clear visual gutter between the sidebar and main content.
Avoid full-bleed cards; they should float on the page background.
Logo row at the top, followed by search, primary navigation, secondary navigation, and account/utility links towards the bottom.
Navigation items are left-aligned, icon + label, with clear active and hover states.
Avoid dense sidebar content; keep labels concise and use icons for quick recognition.
Top summary metrics typically span equal-width columns across the page (e.g., 3 or 4 cards).
Main content area can be split into 7/5 or 8/4 column layouts for charts and supporting modules.
Align card titles and content blocks to the same vertical rhythm to reduce visual noise.

pageHeader.description: Top row containing breadcrumb or app name on the left and context/user actions on the right.

summaryRow.description: Row of concise summary cards for key KPIs such as products tracked, dynamic price updates, competitor signals.

mainContent.description: Primary 2-column layout with a large chart or gauge on the left and supporting metrics on the right.

lowerModules.description: Additional cards and list-like modules stacked below the main content, aligned to the main grid.

AppShell.role: Overall frame containing sidebar, header, and main content area.
structure: Left sidebar with app logo and main navigation.
Main area with top header (page title, avatar) and scrollable content.
do: Keep the shell minimal so that content cards stand out.
Maintain consistent page padding and spacing between major sections.
dont: Do not add heavy borders or strong colored backgrounds around the entire shell.
Do not reduce padding to the point where cards touch the viewport edges.

elements.LogoRow: App logo icon on the left, name in title-sm typography.
elements.SearchField: Compact input with leading search icon, subtle background, and no hard border.
elements.NavGroupLabel: Text styled with label typography and muted color.
elements.NavItem: Row with icon + label, medium padding and pill-shaped hover/active state.
elements.Badge: Small circular count indicator on the right of certain nav items.

NavItem.role: Clickable navigation row inside the sidebar.
rules: Use concise labels and meaningful icons.
Align icon and text vertically centered.
Use a subtle dot or count badge for items with alerts or activity.

TopBar.role: Page-level header within the main content area.

elements.PageSubtitle: body-sm typography, muted color below the main title.
elements.ContextLabel: Small label such as product area name in label typography.
elements.UserAvatar: Circular avatar on the far right.

rules: Use Card as the base building block for all dashboard modules.
Do not mix multiple card styles in the same row; keep them visually consistent.
Allow card height to grow with content but avoid extreme height differences within the same row.

SummaryCard.role: Small card summarizing a single KPI with a sparkline and delta.

elements.Label: Short description of the metric using label or body-sm typography.
elements.PrimaryValue: KPIValue style for the main number.
elements.Delta: DeltaLabel style showing percentage change and direction.
elements.Sparkline: Small, unobtrusive line chart aligned to the right or bottom.

rules: Keep the content minimal and focused on a single metric.
Use semantic colors in the delta label to indicate performance direction.
Avoid large icons or images; this card should feel light and analytical.

GaugeCard.role: Large card containing a radial gauge for demand or health metrics.

elements.Title: title-lg typography at the top-left.
elements.Subtitle: body-sm muted text below the title.
elements.Gauge: Radial gauge arc with colored segments (green, orange, red) and central percentage label.
elements.Legend: Row of color-coded labels explaining each segment.
elements.Description: Short explanatory text about what the gauge measures.

BarChartCard.role: Card containing a vertical bar chart, typically representing revenue over months.

elements.Title: title-lg typography.
elements.Subtitle: body-sm muted text for explanation.
elements.ChartArea: Bars using chart-blue, chart-green, chart-orange, and chart-red.
elements.AxisLabels: body-sm or label typography, subtle color.
elements.Legend: Optional labeled swatches below or above chart.

ListMetricCard.role: Card listing multiple metrics or rules with associated progress or status.

elements.ItemRow: Each row contains a title, short description or tags, and a progress or status indicator.
elements.ProgressBar: Horizontal bar with rounded ends indicating completion.
elements.StatusText: Small text indicating counts or percentage complete.
elements.IconOrBadge: Optional icon or execution count indicator to the right.

ProgressBar.role: Horizontal indicator of completion or utilization.
rules: Use rounded ends and smooth fill.
Avoid thick or multi-segment bars; keep it minimal and focused.
Use success or warning colors when representing health or compliance.

SearchField.role: Compact search input inside the sidebar.

FilterChip.role: Pill-shaped control for filters, such as date range selectors.

KPIValue.role: Number-heavy typographic style for important metrics.
rules: Keep KPI values on a single line where possible.
Pair large KPIs with short labels and, when appropriate, a DeltaLabel.
Use consistent formatting for currencies and separators.

DeltaLabel.role: Small indicator for percentage change vs previous period.
rules: Include an up/down arrow icon when possible.
Use green for positive movement, red for negative, and neutral blue for flat/other changes.
Keep this label compact and close to the main KPI it describes.

PillTag.role: Rounded label for metadata, rule names, or small categories.

Avatar.role: Circular avatar for the user in the top-right of the page.

interaction.description: Interaction guidelines that maintain a calm, confident feel with subtle but clear feedback.

hover.cards: Slight shadow increase and/or soft border-color change; no large color shifts.
hover.navItems: Background becomes slightly tinted and text color increases from secondary to primary.
hover.chips: Background lightens slightly and border (if any) becomes more pronounced.

focus.style: Use a 1–2px outline or ring in primary blue with subtle outer glow.
rules: Focus should always be visible for keyboard users.
Avoid using only color changes for focus; include outline or shadow differences.

active.cards: Typically no distinct active state; rely on hovered/selected styling where relevant.
active.navItems: Use the active background and text colors defined in the Sidebar/NavItem tokens.
active.chips: Increase contrast via stronger background tint and text color.

disabled.style: Reduce opacity, remove shadows, and disable hover/focus elevation.
rules: Disabled text should remain legible but clearly de-emphasized.
Do not rely solely on color to indicate disabled state; pair with cursor and interaction behavior.

motion.description: Animations are subtle and fast, reinforcing responsiveness without drawing undue attention.
usageGuidelines: Apply transitions to opacity, transform (e.g., slight translateY for hover), and shadow.
Avoid large or bouncy animations that might distract from the data.
For charts, animate values and bars in quickly with easing-standard when data first loads.

principles.description: High-level creative direction and design principles that should guide all decisions when extending this system.
statements: Clarity over decoration: Every visual element must serve comprehension of data or navigation, not decoration.
Calm density: The interface can show many metrics at once, but uses generous whitespace, consistent spacing, and clear hierarchy to avoid feeling crowded.
Soft, neutral, professional: Use cool greys and a restrained blue as the main accent; reserve green, red, and orange for semantic meanings like performance or risk.
Consistency first: Reuse card structures, spacing, and typography scales across the app instead of inventing new patterns.
Hierarchy through typography and layout: Emphasize important information with size, weight, and placement rather than bright colors or heavy borders.
Semantic color usage: Blue is primary and neutral, green indicates success, red signals risk or negative change, and orange captures warnings or mixed performance.
Minimal chrome: Navigation and shell elements are understated so the data modules and cards take visual priority.
Friendly but serious: Rounded corners and soft shadows keep the product approachable, but overall composition remains disciplined and business-focused.
